denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed its review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on June 22, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated June 24, 2026.

After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy, we are unable to approve the requested surgical procedure. Plan policy requires that a less costly therapeutic modality be attempted before the requested procedure; a step-down approach using a lower-cost modality is considered appropriate before surgical intervention for this condition, and the records do not establish that this approach has been completed.

This determination also rests on a plan exclusion: your benefit plan excludes coverage for surgical treatment of temporomandibular joint disorders, and that exclusion applies to the requested service.

If you disagree with this decision, you may appeal. Appeals must be submitted to the Cigna Claims Service Center, P.O. Box 5555, Scranton, PA 18505, or by fax to 1-800-555-0143, within the time permitted under your plan.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Medical Review

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old man with refractory internal derangement of the right temporomandibular joint (M26.63): chronic jaw pain, mouth opening limited to 22 mm, and joint locking, on a background of nocturnal bruxism and a remote mandibular injury. MRI shows anterior disc displacement without reduction. He is covered by a fully-insured employer plan issued in his state of residence, which by statute mandates coverage of temporomandibular joint disorders on the same basis as other joint conditions. Step-down therapy has already failed: a stabilization splint worn for six months, physical therapy, scheduled NSAIDs, and two arthrocentesis (joint lavage) procedures, none of which restored opening or relieved the locking. His oral and maxillofacial surgeon then requested TMJ arthroscopy. The practice's first appeal, mailed to the appeals address printed on the denial letter, was returned marked 'wrong department,' which delayed the appeal past the window the letter allowed.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Cigna TMJ step-down denial, a plan TMJ exclusion preempted by a state mandate, and a wrong-department appeals contact are all authentic, exploitable real-world defects; conservative ladder already exhausted.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Generic un-named Cigna Medical Coverage Policy / plan-exclusion references with no fabricated codes/edition; realistically vague.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: State TMJ mandate voids the cited plan exclusion on a fully-insured plan, the step-down basis is factually false (splint/PT/NSAIDs/two arthrocentesis failed), and the misrouted appeal was returned. Easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: State mandate requires TMJ coverage on parity with other joints, voiding the cited plan exclusion for this fully-insured plan
Step-therapy basis is factually false: splint, PT, NSAIDs, and two arthrocenteses already failed without restoring function
Wrong-department appeals contact returned the timely first appeal 'wrong department', supporting a defective-notice deadline extension
Objective medical-necessity evidence (22 mm opening, locking, MRI disc displacement without reduction) was ignored
strong_defenses: The plan can argue arthroscopy is itself a surgical TMJ procedure and that a still-lower-cost modality remained, framing the request as premature surgical escalation under the step-down policy
